lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.

A criticism on Rufinus' Apology to Anastasius. His excuses for not coming to Rome are absurd. His parents are dead and the journey is easy. No one ever heard before of his being imprisoned or exiled for the faith.

metadata.author: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus

title: A criticism on Rufinus' Apology to Anastasius. His excuses for not coming to Rome are absurd. His parents are dead and the journey is easy. No one ever heard before of his being imprisoned or exiled for the faith.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He professes in the first place to be replying to insinuations made at Rome against his orthodoxy, he being a man most fully approved in respect both of divine faith and of charity.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He says that he would have wished to come himself, were it not that he had lately returned, after thirty years’ absence, to his parents, and that it would have seemed harsh and inhuman to leave them after having been so long in coming to them; and also if he had not become somewhat less robust through his long and toilsome journey, and too infirm to begin his labours again.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As he had not been able to come himself, he had sent his apology as a kind of literary cudgel which the bishop might hold in his hand and drive away the dogs who were raging against him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he is a man approved for his divine faith and charity by all, and especially by the Bishop to whom he writes; how is it that at Rome he is assailed and reviled, and that the reports of the attacks upon his reputation grow thicker.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Further, what sort of humility is this, that a man speaks of himself as approved for his divine faith and charity?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The Apostles prayed, “Lord increase our faith,” and received for answer: “If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed;” and even to Peter it is said: “O thou of little faith, wherefore didst thou doubt?”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why should I speak of charity, which is greater than either faith or hope, and which Paul says he hopes for rather than assumes: without which even the blood shed in martyrdom and the body given up to the flames has no reward to crown it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Yet both of these our friend claims as his own: in such a way, however, that there still remain creatures who bark against him, and who will go on barking unless the illustrious Pontiff drives them away with his stick.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But how absurd is this plea which he puts forward, of having returned to his parents after thirty years.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why, he has got neither father nor mother!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He left them alive when he was a young man, and, now that he is old, he pines for them when they are dead.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But perhaps, he means by “parents,” what is meant in the talk of the soldiers and the common people, his kinsfolk and relations; well, he says he does not wish to be thought so harsh and inhuman as to desert them; and therefore he leaves his home and goes to live at Aquileia.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: That most approved faith of his is in great peril at Rome, and yet he lies on his back, being a bit tired after thirty years, and cannot make that very easy journey in a carriage along that Flaminian Way.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He puts forward his lassitude after his long journey, as if he had done nothing but move about for thirty years, or as if, after resting at Aquileia for two years, he was still worn out with the labour of his past travels.
